questions.question: Are all four formal arms complete and comparable?
questions.evidence: Each arm has 64 planned, 64 trajectories and 64 judged tasks with the same task IDs, model, agent, Judge and c10 setting.

questions.question: Was backend order counterbalanced?
questions.evidence: Pair A ran Local then Lexmount; Pair B ran Lexmount then Local.

questions.question: Could Judge transport failures have been counted as browser failures?
questions.evidence: Synthetic Judge failure detection found zero remaining cases in every formal arm; valid artifact-only evaluations remain valid Judge observations.

questions.question: Did Lexmount leak sessions or fail session creation at c10?
questions.evidence: Both Lexmount arms started at zero active sessions, reached 10, ended at zero, reported zero monitor errors and zero session-create task signals.

questions.question: Did Local Chrome leave new browser processes or cross a resource guard?
questions.evidence: Both Local arms ended without a guard trigger; post-run process start-time and cgroup checks found no browser tree created by these arms. Pre-existing unrelated browser trees were not modified.

questions.question: Is 128 attempts a valid independent sample size for uncertainty?
questions.evidence: The two observations for each task are correlated. The reported 95% interval resamples 64 task_id clusters, not 128 attempts.

questions.question: Does this stage prove that Lexmount is better than Local Chrome on LexBench?
questions.evidence: The clustered difference is +1.56 percentage points with a 95% interval from -9.38 to +12.50 points.

questions.question: Does this stage disprove every site-specific Lexmount advantage?
questions.evidence: Five tasks were Lexmount-only in both pairs and four were Local-only in both pairs. Stage 2 repeats these stable and flipping cases.

questions.question: Can runner CPU and PSS establish lower total Lexmount infrastructure cost?
questions.evidence: Lexmount measurements exclude remote browser hosts; they only establish lower load on the runner/controller.

questions.question: Can collected GPU values explain browser quality or throughput?
questions.evidence: GPT-5.5 was served remotely and unrelated host GPU workloads changed between arms. GPU metrics are context-only.

decision: Do not treat the earlier one-shot gap as a browser-only causal effect. Continue with targeted crossover repeats before making mechanism claims.
